exam.title: Sample Exam A
exam.description: A combined practice quiz — all 40 main exam questions plus 26 additional questions, with instant feedback, full rationales, and per-question Learning Objectives.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements describe a valid test objective?

options.text: To prove that there are no unfixed defects in the system under test
To prove that there will be no failures after the implementation of the system into production
To reduce the risk level of the test object and to build confidence in the quality level
To verify that there are no untested combinations of inputs

rationales.a: Incorrect. It is impossible to prove that there are no defects anymore in the system under test (testing principle 1).
rationales.b: Incorrect. See testing principle 7 (absence-of-defects fallacy).
rationales.c: Correct. Testing finds defects and failures which reduces the level of risk and at the same time gives more confidence in the quality level of the test object.
rationales.d: Incorrect. It is impossible to test all combinations of inputs (testing principle 2 — exhaustive testing is impossible).

questions.stem: Which of the following options shows an example of test activities that contribute to success?

options.text: Having testers involved during various software development lifecycle (SDLC) activities will help to detect defects in work products
Testers try not to disturb the developers while coding, so that the developers write better code
Testers collaborating with end users help to improve the quality of defect reports during component integration and system testing
Certified testers will design much better test cases than non-certified testers

rationales.a: Correct. It is important that testers are involved from the beginning of the SDLC. It will increase understanding of design decisions and will detect defects early.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Both developers and testers will have more understanding of each other's work products and how to test the code when they collaborate.
rationales.c: Incorrect. End users will not help the testers in increasing the quality of defect reports; also, users usually do not participate in low-level testing levels like integration testing.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Being certified does not automatically mean that the tester will be better in test design.

questions.stem: You have been assigned as a tester to a team producing a new system incrementally. You have noticed that no changes have been made to the existing regression test cases for several iterations and no new regression defects were identified. Your manager is happy, but you are not. Which testing principle explains your skepticism?

options.text: Tests wear out
Absence-of-defects fallacy
Defects cluster together
Exhaustive testing is impossible

rationales.a: Correct. This principle means that if the same tests are repeated over and over again, eventually these tests no longer find any new defects. This is probably why the tests all passed in this release as well.
rationales.b: Incorrect. This principle is about the mistaken belief that just finding and fixing a large number of defects will ensure the success of a system.
rationales.c: Incorrect. This principle says that a small number of components usually contain most of the defects.
rationales.d: Incorrect. This principle states that testing all combinations of inputs and preconditions is not feasible.

questions.stem: You work in a team that develops a mobile application for food ordering. In the current iteration the team decided to implement the payment functionality. Which of the following activities is a part of test analysis?

options.text: Estimating that testing the integration with the payment service will take 8 person-days
Deciding that the team should test if it is possible to properly share payment between many users
Using boundary value analysis (BVA) to derive the test data for the test cases that check the correct payment processing for the minimum allowed amount to be paid
Analyzing the discrepancy between the actual result and expected result after executing a test case that checks the process of payment with a credit card, and reporting a defect

rationales.a: Incorrect. Estimating the test effort is part of test planning.
rationales.b: Correct. This is an example of defining test conditions which is a part of test analysis.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Using test techniques to derive coverage items is a part of test design.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Reporting defects found during dynamic testing is a part of test execution.

questions.stem:
Which of the following factors have a SIGNIFICANT influence on the test approach?

i. The SDLC
ii. The number of defects detected in previous projects
iii. The identified product risks
iv. New regulatory requirements forcing formal white-box testing
v. The test environment setup

options.text: i, ii have significant influence
i, iii, iv have significant influence
ii, iv, v have significant influence
iii, v have significant influence

rationales.a: Incorrect. ii (defect counts in previous projects) is not as significant as i, iii and iv.
rationales.b: Correct. i (SDLC), iii (identified product risks), and iv (regulatory requirements) are all important factors influencing the test approach.
rationales.c: Incorrect. The test environment (v) has no significant influence on the test approach.
rationales.d: Incorrect. v (test environment) does not have significant influence, and missing items i and iv which do.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following tasks belong MAINLY to a testing role?

options.text: Configure test environments
Maintain the product backlog
Design solutions to new requirements
Create the test plan
Analyze the test basis

rationales.a: Correct. Configuring test environments is done by the testers.
rationales.b: Incorrect. The product backlog is built and maintained by the product owner.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Designing solutions is done by the development team.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Creating the test plan is a managerial role.
rationales.e: Correct. Analyzing the test basis is done by the testers as part of test analysis.

questions.stem:
Which of the following skills (i-v) are the MOST important skills of a tester?

i. Having domain knowledge
ii. Creating a product vision
iii. Being a good team player
iv. Planning and organizing the work of the team
v. Critical thinking

options.text: ii and iv are important
i, iii and v are important
i, ii and v are important
iii and iv are important

rationales.a: Incorrect. Creating a product vision (ii) is a business analyst task; planning team work (iv) is a test manager / whole team task.
rationales.b: Correct. Domain knowledge (i), being a team player (iii), and critical thinking (v) are core tester skills.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Creating a product vision (ii) is not a tester skill.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Planning team work (iv) is not primarily a tester skill.

questions.stem: How is the whole team approach present in the interactions between testers and business representatives?

options.text: Business representatives decide on test automation approaches
Testers help business representatives to define a test strategy
Business representatives are not part of the whole team approach
Testers help business representatives to create suitable acceptance tests

rationales.a: Incorrect. The test automation approach is defined by testers with the help of developers and business representatives.
rationales.b: Incorrect. The test strategy is decided in collaboration with the developers.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Testers, developers, and business representatives are all part of the whole team approach.
rationales.d: Correct. Testers work closely with business representatives to ensure desired quality levels, including helping create suitable acceptance tests.

questions.stem: Consider the following rule: "for every SDLC activity there is a corresponding test activity". In which SDLC models does this rule hold?

options.text: Only in sequential development models
Only in iterative development models
Only in iterative and incremental development models
In sequential, incremental, and iterative development models

rationales.a: Incorrect. The rule holds for more than just sequential models.
rationales.b: Incorrect. The rule holds for more than just iterative models.
rationales.c: Incorrect. The rule holds for sequential models too.
rationales.d: Correct. This rule holds for all SDLC models.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements BEST describes the acceptance test-driven development (ATDD) approach?

options.text: In ATDD, acceptance criteria are typically created based on the given/when/then format
In ATDD, test cases are mainly created at component testing and are code-oriented
In ATDD, tests are created, based on acceptance criteria to drive the development of the related software
In ATDD, tests are based on the desired behavior of the software, which makes it easier for team members to understand them

rationales.a: Incorrect. given/when/then is more often used in behavior-driven development (BDD).
rationales.b: Incorrect. That is the description of test-driven development (TDD).
rationales.c: Correct. In ATDD tests are written from acceptance criteria as part of the design process.
rationales.d: Incorrect. That description fits BDD.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an example of the shift-left approach?

options.text: Reviewing the user requirements before they are formally accepted by the stakeholders
Writing a component test before the corresponding code is written
Executing a performance efficiency test for a component during component testing
Writing a test script before setting up the configuration management process

rationales.a: Incorrect (this IS shift-left). Early review is an example of the shift-left approach.
rationales.b: Incorrect (this IS shift-left). TDD is an example of the shift-left approach.
rationales.c: Incorrect (this IS shift-left). Early non-functional testing is an example of the shift-left approach.
rationales.d: Correct. Test scripts should be subject to configuration management, so it makes no sense to create the test scripts before this process is set up.

questions.stem: Which of the arguments below would you use to convince your manager to organize retrospectives at the end of each release cycle?

options.text: Retrospectives are very popular these days and clients would appreciate it if we added them to our processes
Organizing retrospectives will save the organization money because without them end user representatives do not provide immediate feedback about the product
Process weaknesses identified during the retrospective can be analyzed and serve as a to do list for the organization's continuous process improvement program
Retrospectives embrace five values including courage and respect, which are crucial to maintain continuous improvement in the organization

rationales.a: Incorrect. Retrospectives have little importance for clients; they identify improvement opportunities.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Retrospectives are not aimed at collecting product feedback, but process feedback, and are internal to the team.
rationales.c: Correct. Regularly conducted retrospectives, with appropriate follow-up activities, are critical to continual improvement of development and testing.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Courage and respect are values of Extreme Programming, not closely related to retrospectives.

questions.stem:
Which types of failures (1-4) fit which test levels (A-D) BEST?

1. Failures in system behavior as it deviates from the user's business needs
2. Failures in communication between components
3. Failures in logic in the code
4. Failures in not correctly implemented business rules

A. Component testing
B. Component integration testing
C. System testing
D. Acceptance testing

rationales.a: Correct. User business needs → acceptance testing (1D); component communication → component integration testing (2B); logic failures → component testing (3A); business rules → system testing (4C).
rationales.b: Incorrect. Mismatches business rules and logic failures with the wrong levels.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Multiple mismatches.
rationales.d: Incorrect. User business needs belong to acceptance testing, not system testing.

questions.stem:
You are testing a user story with three acceptance criteria: AC1, AC2 and AC3. AC1 is covered by test case TC1, AC2 by TC2, and AC3 by TC3. The test execution history had three test runs on three consecutive versions of the software as follows:

            Execution 1   Execution 2   Execution 3
TC1:        (1) Failed    (4) Passed    (7) Passed
TC2:        (2) Passed    (5) Failed    (8) Passed
TC3:        (3) Failed    (6) Failed    (9) Passed

Tests are repeated once you are informed that all defects found in the test run are corrected and a new version of the software is available. Which of the above tests are executed as regression tests?

options.text: Only 5, 7
Only 5, 6

rationales.a: Incorrect. Tests 4, 8, 9 follow failed runs of the same TC and are confirmation tests, not regression.
rationales.b: Correct. TC2 passed in Execution 1 → test (5) is a regression test. TC1 passed in Execution 2 → test (7) is also a regression test. The others are confirmation tests.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Mixes confirmation tests with regression tests.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Test (6) re-runs after a failure → it's a confirmation test, not regression.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of static testing?

options.text: Having less expensive defect management due to the ease of detecting defects later in the SDLC
Fixing defects found during static testing is generally much less expensive than fixing defects found during dynamic testing
Finding coding defects that might not have been found by only performing dynamic testing
Detecting gaps and inconsistencies in requirements

rationales.a: Correct. Defect management is no less expensive — finding and fixing defects later in the SDLC is MORE costly, not less. This statement is backwards.
rationales.b: Incorrect (this IS a benefit of static testing).
rationales.c: Incorrect (this IS a benefit of static testing).
rationales.d: Incorrect (this IS a benefit of static testing).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a benefit of early and frequent feedback?

options.text: It improves the test process for future projects
It forces customers to prioritize their requirements based on agreed risks
It provides a measure for the quality of changes
It helps avoid requirements misunderstandings

rationales.a: Incorrect. Improving future projects does not require feedback to be early or frequent.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Feedback is not used to prioritize requirements.
rationales.c: Incorrect. There is no single recommended way to measure quality of changes; not a listed benefit.
rationales.d: Correct. Early and frequent feedback can prevent misunderstandings about requirements.

questions.stem:
The reviews being used in your organization have the following attributes:

• There is the role of a scribe
• The main purpose is to evaluate quality
• The meeting is led by the author of the work product
• There is individual preparation
• A review report is produced

Which of the following review types is MOST likely being used?

options.text: Informal review
Walkthrough
Technical review
Inspection

rationales.a: Incorrect. A scribe role is specified for walkthroughs, technical reviews, and inspections — not informal reviews.
rationales.b: Correct. The author leading the meeting is allowed only in walkthroughs (or informal reviews), and evaluating quality is a key walkthrough objective.
rationales.c: Incorrect. In technical reviews the author does not typically lead the meeting.
rationales.d: Incorrect. In inspections the author does not lead the meeting — a trained moderator does.

questions.stem: Which of these statements is NOT a factor that contributes to successful reviews?

options.text: Participants should dedicate adequate time for the review
Splitting large work products into small parts to make the required effort less intense
Participants should avoid behaviors that might indicate boredom, exasperation, or hostility to other participants
Failures found should be acknowledged, appreciated, and handled objectively

rationales.a: Incorrect (this IS a success factor).
rationales.b: Incorrect (this IS a success factor).
rationales.c: Incorrect (this IS a success factor).
rationales.d: Correct. During reviews one can find DEFECTS, not failures. The word 'failures' makes this statement incorrect.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a characteristic of experience-based test techniques?

options.text: Test cases are created based on detailed design information
Items tested within the interface code section are used to measure coverage
The test techniques heavily rely on the tester's knowledge of the software and the business domain
The test cases are used to identify deviations from the requirements

rationales.a: Incorrect. That is a characteristic of white-box techniques.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Coverage measured against internal structure is a white-box characteristic.
rationales.c: Correct. Experience-based techniques use the tester's knowledge of expected use, environment, likely defects, and defect distribution.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Identifying deviations from requirements is a black-box characteristic.

questions.stem:
You are testing a simplified apartment search form which has only two search criteria:

• floor (with three possible options: ground floor; first floor; second or higher floor)
• garden type (with three possible options: no garden; small garden; large garden)

Each of the apartments on the ground floor has a garden, apartments on higher floors don't. The form has a built-in validation mechanism that will not allow you to use the search criteria which violate this rule.

Each test has two input values: floor and garden type. You want to apply equivalence partitioning (EP) to cover each floor and each garden type in your tests. What is the minimal number of test cases to achieve 100% EP coverage for valid partitions?

rationales.a: Incorrect. 3 test cases are not enough to cover both 'small garden' and 'large garden' (each can only pair with 'ground floor').
rationales.b: Correct. Need: TC1 (ground, small garden), TC2 (ground, large garden), TC3 (first floor, no garden), TC4 (second+ floor, no garden) = 4 test cases.
rationales.c: Incorrect. 5 is more than the minimum needed.
rationales.d: Incorrect. 6 is more than the minimum needed.

questions.stem:
You are testing a system that calculates the final course grade. Rules: 0-50 = failed; 51-60 = fair; 61-70 = satisfactory; 71-80 = good; 81-90 = very good; 91-100 = excellent.

Test cases prepared:
TC1: 91 (excellent), TC2: 50 (failed), TC3: 81 (very good), TC4: 60 (fair), TC5: 70 (satisfactory), TC6: 80 (good).

What is the 2-value boundary value analysis (BVA) coverage for the final result that is achieved with the existing test cases?

rationales.a: Correct. There are 12 boundary values: 0, 50, 51, 60, 61, 70, 71, 80, 81, 90, 91, 100. The test cases cover 6 of them (50, 60, 70, 80, 81, 91). 6/12 = 50%.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect.
rationales.d: Incorrect. 100% would require covering all 12 boundary values.

questions.stem:
A bicycle rental store introduced a new CRM:
• Anyone can rent, but members get a 20% discount
• If the return deadline is missed, the discount is no longer available
• After 15 rentals, members get a gift: a T-Shirt

Decision table:
Conditions:           R1 R2 R3 R4 R5 R6 R7 R8
Being a member:       T  T  T  T  F  F  F  F
Missed deadline:      T  F  T  F  T  F  F  T
15th rental:          F  F  T  T  F  F  T  T
Actions:
20% discount:            X        X
Gift T-Shirt:               X  X              X

Based ONLY on the feature description, which rule describes an impossible situation?

options.text: R4
R2
R6
R8

rationales.a: Incorrect. R4 (member, no missed deadline, 15th rental) → discount + T-Shirt is correct.
rationales.b: Incorrect. R2 (member, no missed deadline) → discount is correct.
rationales.c: Incorrect. R6 (non-member) → no discount is correct.
rationales.d: Correct. R8 shows non-member + missed deadline + 15th rental gets a T-Shirt — but only members can receive a T-Shirt. This action is not correct.

questions.stem:
You test a system whose lifecycle is modeled by a state transition diagram. The system starts in INIT and ends in OFF. The transitions are:

• INIT --test--> DEBUG MODE
• INIT --run--> IN OPERATION
• DEBUG MODE --done--> OFF
• IN OPERATION --error--> DEBUG MODE
• IN OPERATION --pause--> ON HOLD
• IN OPERATION --done--> OFF
• ON HOLD --resume--> IN OPERATION

What is the MINIMAL number of test cases to achieve valid transitions coverage?

rationales.a: Incorrect. 4 is more than needed.
rationales.b: Incorrect. 2 is not enough to cover all transitions.
rationales.c: Incorrect. 7 is far more than needed.
rationales.d: Correct. 'test' and 'error' cannot occur in one test case (they lead from different states to DEBUG MODE), and the two 'done' transitions are mutually exclusive. Three test cases suffice, e.g. TC1: test, done; TC2: run, error, done; TC3: run, pause, resume, pause, done.

questions.stem: Your test suite achieved 100% statement coverage. What is the consequence of this fact?

options.text: Each instruction in the code that contains a defect has been executed at least once
Any test suite containing more test cases than your test suite will also achieve 100% statement coverage
Each path in the code has been executed at least once
Every combination of input values has been tested at least once

rationales.a: Correct. Since 100% statement coverage is achieved, every statement, including ones with defects, must have been executed at least once.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Coverage depends on what is tested, not on the number of test cases.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Loops can create infinite possible paths; path coverage is not equal to statement coverage.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Exhaustive testing is impossible.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT true for white-box testing?

options.text: During white-box testing the entire software implementation is considered
White-box coverage metrics can help identify additional tests to increase code coverage
White-box test techniques can be used in static testing
White-box testing can help identify gaps in requirements implementation

rationales.a: Incorrect (this IS true). White-box considers the entire implementation.
rationales.b: Incorrect (this IS true). White-box coverage metrics objectively guide additional tests.
rationales.c: Incorrect (this IS true). White-box techniques can be used in reviews (static testing).
rationales.d: Correct. This is the weakness of white-box: it cannot identify missing implementation because it is based on code structure, not on the requirements specification.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the concept behind error guessing?

options.text: Error guessing involves using your knowledge and experience of defects found in the past and typical errors made by developers
Error guessing involves using your personal experience of development and the errors you made as a developer
Error guessing requires you to imagine that you are the user of the test object and to guess errors the user could make interacting with it
Error guessing requires you to rapidly duplicate the development task to identify the sort of errors a developer might make

rationales.a: Correct. Error guessing relies on past experience with defects and typical developer errors (sometimes supported by checklists).
rationales.b: Incorrect. Personal dev experience helps, but error guessing is not based on prior development knowledge specifically.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Error guessing is not a usability technique about user interaction errors.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Duplicating the development task is impractical and is not what error guessing is.

questions.stem: In your project there has been a delay in the release of a brand-new application and test execution started late, but you have very detailed domain knowledge and good analytical skills. The full list of requirements has not yet been shared with the team, but management is asking for some test results to be presented. Which test technique fits BEST in this situation?

options.text: Checklist-based testing
Error guessing
Exploratory testing
Branch testing

rationales.a: Incorrect. New product means you likely don't have a checklist yet.
rationales.b: Incorrect. New product means you don't have enough info to make correct error guesses.
rationales.c: Correct. Exploratory testing is most useful when there are few known specifications and/or there is a pressing timeline.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Branch testing requires code and is time-consuming; it doesn't leverage domain knowledge.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the way acceptance criteria can be documented?

options.text: Performing retrospectives to determine the actual needs of the stakeholders regarding a given user story
Using the given/when/then format to describe an example test condition related to a given user story
Using verbal communication to reduce the risk of misunderstanding the acceptance criteria by others
Documenting risks related to a given user story in a test plan to facilitate the risk-based testing of a given user story

rationales.a: Incorrect. Retrospectives capture lessons learned, not acceptance criteria.
rationales.b: Correct. given/when/then is the standard way to document acceptance criteria.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Verbal communication doesn't physically document the criteria (the 'card' aspect of the 3 C's).
rationales.d: Incorrect. Acceptance criteria are related to a user story, not a test plan, and risks are not such conditions.

questions.stem:
Consider the following user story:

   As an Editor
   I want to review content before it is published
   so that I can ensure the grammar is correct

Acceptance criteria:
• The user can log in to the content management system with "Editor" role
• The editor can view existing content pages
• The editor can edit the page content
• The editor can add markup comments
• The editor can save changes
• The editor can reassign to the "content owner" role to make updates

Which of the following is the BEST example of an ATDD test for this user story?

options.text: Test if the editor can save the document after edit the page content
Test if the content owner can log in and make updates to the content
Test if the editor can schedule the edited content for publication
Test if the editor can reassign to another editor to make updates

rationales.a: Correct. This test covers two acceptance criteria: editing the document and saving changes.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Acceptance criteria cover editor activities, not content owner activities.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Scheduling for publication is not covered by the acceptance criteria.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Acceptance criteria state reassigning to the 'content owner' role, not to another editor.

questions.stem: How do testers add value to iteration and release planning?

options.text: Testers determine the priority of the user stories to be developed
Testers focus only on the functional aspects of the system to be tested
Testers participate in the detailed risk identification and risk assessment of user stories
Testers guarantee the release of high-quality software through early test design during the release planning

rationales.a: Incorrect. Priorities are determined by the business representative together with the development team.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Testers focus on both functional and non-functional aspects.
rationales.c: Correct. According to the syllabus, this is one of the ways testers add value to iteration and release planning.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Early test design is not part of release planning and does not automatically guarantee quality.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following options are the exit criteria for testing a system?

options.text: Test environment readiness
The ability to log in to the test object by the tester
Estimated defect density is reached
Requirements are translated into given/when/then format
Regression tests are automated

rationales.a: Incorrect. Test environment readiness is a resource availability criterion — entry criteria.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Resource availability criterion — entry criteria.
rationales.c: Correct. Estimated defect density is a measure of diligence — exit criteria.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Requirements translated to testable format — entry criteria.
rationales.e: Correct. Automation of regression tests is a completion criterion — exit criteria.

questions.stem:
Your team uses the three-point estimation technique to estimate the test effort for a new high-risk feature:

• Most optimistic: 2 person-hours
• Most likely: 11 person-hours
• Most pessimistic: 14 person-hours

What is the final estimate?

options.text: 9 person-hours
14 person-hours
11 person-hours
10 person-hours

rationales.a: Incorrect.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect.
rationales.d: Correct. E = (optimistic + 4 × most likely + pessimistic) / 6 = (2 + 44 + 14) / 6 = 60/6 = 10.

questions.stem:
Mobile restaurant-finder app test cases:

TC 001 (priority 3, no dependency) — Select type of food
TC 002 (priority 2, depends on TC 001) — Select restaurant
TC 003 (priority 1, depends on TC 002) — Get direction
TC 004 (priority 2, depends on TC 002) — Call restaurant
TC 005 (priority 3, depends on TC 002) — Make reservation

Which test case should be executed as the third one?

options.text: TC 003
TC 005
TC 002
TC 001

rationales.a: Correct. Order: TC 001 (dependency), TC 002 (dependency), then TC 003 (highest priority), then TC 004, then TC 005.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect. TC 002 must execute second to satisfy dependencies.
rationales.d: Incorrect. TC 001 must execute first.

questions.stem:
Consider:
1. Usability testing
2. Component testing
3. Functional testing
4. Reliability testing

A. Q1: technology facing, supporting the team
B. Q2: business facing, supporting the team
C. Q3: business facing, critique the product
D. Q4: technology facing, critique the product

How do the test categories map onto the agile testing quadrants?

rationales.a: Correct. Usability → Q3 (1-C); Component → Q1 (2-A); Functional → Q2 (3-B); Reliability → Q4 (4-D).
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect.
rationales.d: Incorrect.

questions.stem:
During risk analysis:

• Risk: Response time is too long to generate a report
• Risk likelihood: medium; risk impact: high
• Response: An independent test team performs performance efficiency testing during system testing; a selected sample of end users performs alpha and beta testing before release

What measure is proposed in response to this risk?

options.text: Risk acceptance
Contingency plan
Risk mitigation
Risk transfer

rationales.a: Incorrect. The risk is not accepted — concrete actions are proposed.
rationales.b: Incorrect. No contingency plans are proposed.
rationales.c: Correct. The proposed actions are testing actions, which is a form of risk mitigation.
rationales.d: Incorrect. The risk is not transferred but mitigated.

questions.stem: Which work product can be used by an agile team to show the amount of work completed and total work remaining for a given iteration?

options.text: Acceptance criteria
Defect report
Test completion report
Burndown chart

rationales.a: Incorrect. Acceptance criteria show readiness, not progress.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Defect reports report defects, not progress.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Test completion reports come at the end, not continuously.
rationales.d: Correct. Burndown charts graphically represent work left vs time remaining, updated daily.

questions.stem: You need to update an automated test script for a new requirement. Which process indicates that you create a new version of the test script in the test repository?

options.text: Traceability management
Maintenance testing
Configuration management
Requirements engineering

rationales.a: Incorrect. Traceability is the relationship between two or more work products, not between versions of the same one.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Maintenance testing tests changes; it is not version control.
rationales.c: Correct. Configuration management involves the version control of all test items.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Requirements engineering is about requirements, not test script versioning.

questions.stem:
You received the following defect report from the developers, stating that the anomaly is not reproducible:

   Application hangs up
   2022-May-03 — John Doe — Rejected
   The application hangs up after entering "Test input: $ä" in the Name field on the new user creation screen. Tried to log off, log in with test_admin01 account, same issue. Tried with other test admin accounts, same issue. No error message received; log contains fatal error notification. Based on the test case TC-1305, the application should accept the provided input and create the user. Please fix with high priority, this feature is related to REQ-0012, which is a critical new business requirement.

What critical information is MISSING from this report that would have been useful for the developers?

options.text: Expected result and actual result
References and defect status
Test environment and test item
Priority and severity

rationales.a: Incorrect. Expected ('should accept...') and actual ('hangs up after entering...') results are both present.
rationales.b: Incorrect. References to test case TC-1305 and requirement REQ-0012 are present, and the status is 'Rejected'.
rationales.c: Correct. We don't know in which test environment the anomaly was detected, nor which application (and its version) is affected.
rationales.d: Incorrect. The report indicates high priority for a critical business requirement.

questions.stem: Which test activity does a data preparation tool support?

options.text: Test monitoring and test control
Test analysis
Test design and test implementation
Test completion

rationales.a: Incorrect. Monitoring/control checks progress; no test data is prepared.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Analysis identifies and prioritizes conditions; test data is not prepared.
rationales.c: Correct. Test design and implementation can include the identification, creation, or acquisition of testware necessary for test execution (e.g., test data).
rationales.d: Incorrect. Test completion happens at milestones — too late for preparing test data.

questions.stem: Which item correctly identifies a potential risk of performing test automation?

options.text: It may introduce unknown regressions in production
Sufficient efforts to maintain testware may not be properly allocated
Testing tools and associated testware may not be sufficiently relied upon
It may reduce the time allocated for manual testing

rationales.a: Incorrect. Test automation does not introduce unknown regressions in production.
rationales.b: Correct. Wrong allocation of effort to maintain testware is a real risk of test automation.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Test tools must be selected so they CAN be relied upon — that's a requirement, not a risk.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Reducing manual testing is a benefit of automation, not a risk.

questions.stem: You were given a task to analyze and fix causes of failures in a new system to be released. Which activity are you performing?

options.text: Debugging
Software testing
Requirement elicitation
Defect management

rationales.a: Correct. Debugging is the process of finding, analyzing, and removing the causes of failures in a component or system.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Testing is about planning, preparation, and evaluation — not fixing causes of failures.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Requirement elicitation gathers requirements.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Defect management is about recognizing, recording, classifying, investigating, resolving, and disposing of defects.

questions.stem: In many software organizations the test department is called the Quality Assurance (QA) department. Is this sentence correct or not and why?

options.text: It is correct. Testing and QA mean exactly the same thing
It is correct. These names can be used interchangeably because both testing and QA focus their activities on the same quality issues
It is not correct. Testing is something more; testing includes all activities with regard to quality. QA focuses on quality-related processes
It is not correct. QA is focused on quality-related processes while testing concentrates on demonstrating that a component or system is fit for purpose and to detect defects

rationales.a: Incorrect. Testing and QA are not the same.
rationales.b: Incorrect. They focus on different things.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Testing is NOT broader than QA — they are distinct.
rationales.d: Correct. QA is focused on establishing, introducing, monitoring, improving, and adhering to quality-related processes, while testing focuses on evaluating components/systems to detect defects and demonstrate fit for purpose.

questions.stem: A phone ringing in a neighboring cubicle distracts a programmer causing him to improperly program the logic that checks the upper boundary of an input variable. Later, during system testing, a tester notices that this input field accepts invalid input values. Which of the following correctly describes an incorrectly coded upper bound?

options.text: The root cause
A failure
An error
A defect

rationales.a: Incorrect. The root cause is the distraction (the phone ringing) leading to the error.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Accepting invalid inputs is the failure (the observable problem).
rationales.c: Incorrect. The error is the mistake in thinking that put the defect in the code.
rationales.d: Correct. The incorrectly coded upper bound (the problem in the code itself) is a defect.

questions.stem:
Consider the following testware:

   Test Charter #04.018   Session time: 1h
   Explore: Registration page
   With: Different sets of incorrect input data
   To discover: Defects related to accepting the registration process with the incorrect input

Which test activity produces this testware as an output?

options.text: Test planning
Test monitoring and test control
Test analysis
Test design

rationales.a: Incorrect.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect.
rationales.d: Correct. Test charters are the output from test design.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the BEST example of how traceability supports testing?

options.text: Performing the impact analysis of a change will give information about the completion of the tests
Analyzing the traceability between test cases and test results will give information about the estimated level of residual risk
Performing the impact analysis of a change will help selecting the right test cases for regression testing
Analyzing the traceability between the test basis, the test objects and the test cases will help in selecting test data to achieve the assumed coverage of the test object

rationales.a: Incorrect. Impact analysis doesn't reveal completeness; it helps select test cases.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Traceability doesn't give residual risk information unless test cases are traced to risks.
rationales.c: Correct. Performing impact analysis of changes helps in selecting the right test cases for the regression test.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Selecting test data is more related to test analysis and implementation, not traceability.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST explains a benefit of independence of testing?

options.text: The use of an independent test team allows project management to assign responsibility for the quality of the final deliverable to the test team

If a test team external to the organization can be afforded, then there are distinct benefits in terms of this external team not being so easily swayed by the delivery concerns of project management and the need to meet strict delivery deadlines

An independent test team can work separately from the developers, need not be distracted with project requirement changes, and can restrict communication with the developers to defect reporting through the defect management system

When specifications contain ambiguities and inconsistencies, assumptions are made on their interpretation, and an independent tester can be useful in questioning those assumptions and the interpretation made by the developer

rationales.a: Incorrect. Quality is everyone's responsibility, not solely the test team's.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Missing deadlines isn't a benefit, and external teams should still respect them.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Working in isolation is bad practice.
rationales.d: Correct. An independent tester is useful in questioning the assumptions and interpretations made by the developer.

questions.stem: You are working as a tester in a team that follows the V-model. Which TWO of the following activities CAN be performed in the initial phases of the SDLC?

options.text: Dynamic test execution
Static testing
Test planning
Acceptance test execution
Maintenance testing

rationales.a: Incorrect. Dynamic test execution needs executable code, created in later phases.
rationales.b: Correct. In sequential models, testers participate in requirement reviews early — a form of static testing.
rationales.c: Correct. Test planning can be performed early in the SDLC alongside analysis and design.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Acceptance test execution requires a working product, delivered later in V-model.
rationales.e: Incorrect. Maintenance testing happens when there is a working and deployed product.

questions.stem:
Which of the following are advantages of DevOps?

i. Faster product release and faster time to market
ii. Increases the need for repetitive manual testing
iii. Constant availability of executable software
iv. Reduction in the number of regression tests associated with code refactoring
v. Setting up the test automation framework is inexpensive since everything is automated

options.text: i, ii, iv are advantages
iii, v are advantages
i, iii are advantages
ii, iv, v are advantages

rationales.a: Incorrect. DevOps decreases (not increases) manual testing needs.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Setting up test automation framework is expensive, not inexpensive.
rationales.c: Correct. i (faster release/time-to-market) and iii (constant executable availability) are true DevOps advantages.
rationales.d: Incorrect. ii, iv, v are all false claims.

questions.stem: You work as a tester on a mobile food-ordering app. One client requirement states: "The order must be processed in less than 10 seconds in 95% of the cases". You ran a set of test cases that issued random orders, measured the processing time, and checked results against the requirement. What test type did you perform?

options.text: Functional, because the test cases cover the user's business requirement for the system
Non-functional, because they measure the system's performance
Functional, because the test cases interact with the user interface
White-box, because we need to know the internal structure of the program to measure the order processing time

rationales.a: Incorrect. Functional tests check WHAT a system does. This checks HOW (how fast).
rationales.b: Correct. This is an example of performance efficiency testing — a non-functional test type.
rationales.c: Incorrect. We don't know UI interaction is part of the test, and the main objective is performance.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Performance testing doesn't require structural knowledge.

questions.stem: Your organization's test strategy suggests that once a system is going to be retired, data migration shall be tested. As part of what test type is this testing MOST likely to be performed?

options.text: Maintenance testing
Regression testing
Reliability testing
Integration testing

rationales.a: Correct. When a system is retired, testing data migration is a form of maintenance testing.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Regression testing verifies a fix didn't affect other parts; this scenario is about data migration.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Portability/reliability testing focuses on transferring to another environment.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Integration testing focuses on interactions, not migration, and is a test level not a test type.

questions.stem:
The following is a list of work products produced in the SDLC.

i. Business requirements
ii. Schedule
iii. Test budget
iv. Third-party executable code
v. User stories and their acceptance criteria

Which of them can be reviewed?

options.text: i and iv can be reviewed
i, ii, iii and iv can be reviewed
i, ii, iii, and v can be reviewed
iii, iv, v can be reviewed

rationales.a: Incorrect.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Correct. Only third-party executable code cannot be reviewed. All other items (requirements, schedule, budget, user stories with acceptance criteria) can be reviewed.
rationales.d: Incorrect.

questions.stem:
Decide which of the following statements (i-v) are true for static testing.

i. Abnormal external behaviors are easier to identify with this testing
ii. Discrepancies from a coding standard are easier to find with this testing
iii. It identifies failures caused by defects when the software is run
iv. Its test objective is to identify defects as early as possible
v. Missing coverage for critical security requirements is easier to find and fix

options.text: i, iv, v are true for static testing
i, iii, iv are true for static testing
ii, iii are true for static testing
ii, iv, v are true for static testing

rationales.a: Incorrect. i is about runtime behavior — dynamic testing.
rationales.b: Incorrect. i and iii are dynamic-testing characteristics.
rationales.c: Incorrect. iii (running software) is dynamic, not static.
rationales.d: Correct. ii (deviations from standards) and v (gaps in coverage) are typical static-testing finds; iv (early defect detection) is a shared objective.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about formal reviews is TRUE?

options.text: Some reviews do not require more than one role
The review process has several activities
Documentation to be reviewed is not distributed before the review meeting, with the exception of the work product for specific review types
Defects found during the review are not reported since they are not found by dynamic testing

rationales.a: Incorrect. All review types involve more than one role, even informal ones.
rationales.b: Correct. The formal review process has several activities.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Documentation to be reviewed should be distributed as early as possible.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Defects found during reviews should be reported.

questions.stem: What task may management take on during a formal review?

options.text: Taking overall responsibility for the review
Deciding what is to be reviewed
Ensuring the effective running of review meetings, and moderating, if necessary
Recording review information such as review decisions

rationales.a: Incorrect. Overall responsibility belongs to the review leader.
rationales.b: Correct. Deciding what is to be reviewed is the task of management in a formal review.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Ensuring effective running and moderating is the moderator's task.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Recording is the scribe's task.

questions.stem:
A wine storage system measures temperature T (in °C, rounded to nearest degree). Rules:
• If T = 12 → "optimal temperature"
• If T < 12 → "temperature is too low!"
• If T > 12 → "temperature is too high!"

Using 3-point boundary value analysis (BVA), what is the MINIMAL set of test inputs that achieves 100% of the desired coverage?

rationales.a: Incorrect. Misses the neighbors 10 and 14.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Misses 11 and 13.
rationales.c: Correct. Partitions are {...,10,11}, {12}, {13,14,...}. Boundaries: 11, 12, 13. For 3-point BVA, each boundary + both neighbors → {10, 11, 12, 13, 14}.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Missing 12.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about branch testing is CORRECT?

options.text: If a program includes only unconditional branches, then 100% branch coverage can be achieved without executing any test cases
If the test cases exercise all unconditional branches in the code, then 100% branch coverage is achieved
If 100% statement coverage is achieved, then 100% branch coverage is also achieved
If 100% branch coverage is achieved, then all decision outcomes in each decision statement in the code are exercised

rationales.a: Incorrect. At least one test case is needed to cover even an unconditional branch.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Covering only unconditional branches doesn't imply covering conditional branches.
rationales.c: Incorrect. 100% branch coverage implies 100% statement coverage, NOT the other way around.
rationales.d: Correct. Each decision outcome corresponds to a conditional branch, so 100% branch coverage implies 100% coverage of decision outcomes.

questions.stem: You are testing a banking mobile app. You evaluate each screen and field against a general list of user interface best practices derived from a popular book that maximizes usability for such applications. Which option BEST categorizes the test technique you are using?

options.text: Black-box
Exploratory
Checklist-based
Error guessing

rationales.a: Incorrect. The book is not a formal specification.
rationales.b: Incorrect. The list resembles test conditions rather than test charters.
rationales.c: Correct. The list of UI best practices is a list of test conditions to be systematically checked — checklist-based testing.
rationales.d: Incorrect. The tests focus on usability knowledge, not on guessing failures.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the collaborative approach to user story writing?

options.text: User stories are created by testers and developers and then accepted by business representatives
User stories are created by business representatives, developers, and testers together
User stories are created by business representatives and verified by developers and testers
User stories are created in a way that they are independent, negotiable, valuable, estimable, small, and testable

rationales.a: Incorrect. Excludes business representatives from creation.
rationales.b: Correct. Collaborative user story writing means all stakeholders create user stories together to obtain a shared vision.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Devs and testers also create, not just verify.
rationales.d: Incorrect. That's the INVEST list of properties, not the collaboration approach.

questions.stem:
Consider the following part of a test plan:

   Testing will be performed using component testing and component integration testing. The regulations require to demonstrate that 100% branch coverage is achieved for each component classified as critical.

Which part of the test plan does this part belong to?

options.text: Communication
Risk register
Context of testing
Test approach

rationales.a: Incorrect.
rationales.b: Incorrect.
rationales.c: Incorrect.
rationales.d: Correct. The paragraph contains information on test levels and exit criteria, which are part of the test approach.

questions.stem:
Your team uses planning poker. Rule: if no time to reach full agreement and variation is small, apply rules like "accept the number with the most votes".

Round 1: 21, 2, 5, 34, 13, 8, 2
Round 2: 13, 8, 8, 34, 13, 8, 5
Round 3: 13, 8, 13, 13, 13, 13, 8

Which is the BEST next step?

options.text: The product owner has to step in and make a final decision
Accept 13 as the final test estimate as this has most of the votes
No further action is needed. Consensus has been reached
Remove the new feature from the current release because consensus has not been reached

rationales.a: Incorrect. This should be a team activity, not overruled by one member.
rationales.b: Correct. With small variation, the team's rule says accept the number with the most votes — 13.
rationales.c: Incorrect. There is no full consensus yet.
rationales.d: Incorrect. A feature shouldn't be removed only because of estimation disagreement.

questions.stem: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the test pyramid?

options.text: It emphasizes having far more tests at the lower test levels
It suggests that each low-level test checks a large part of the functionality
It describes distribution of test types across the SDLC
It has no impact on the construction of automated tests

rationales.a: Correct. The test pyramid emphasizes having a larger number of tests at the lower test levels.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Lower-level tests are more atomic, oriented to specific logic — the opposite.
rationales.c: Incorrect. The pyramid shows distribution across test levels, not test types or SDLC phases.
rationales.d: Incorrect. The pyramid model supports the team in test automation.

questions.stem: During risk analysis the team considered the following risk: "The system allows too high a discount for a customer". The team estimated the risk impact to be very high. What can one say about the risk likelihood?

options.text: It is also very high. High risk impact always implies high risk likelihood
It is very low. High risk impact always implies low risk likelihood
One cannot say anything about risk likelihood. Risk impact and risk likelihood are independent.
Risk likelihood is not important with such a high-risk impact. One does not need to define it.

rationales.a: Incorrect. Impact and likelihood are independent.
rationales.b: Incorrect. Impact and likelihood are independent.
rationales.c: Correct. Risk impact and risk likelihood are independent of each other.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Both factors are needed to calculate risk level.

questions.stem:
The following list contains risks identified for a new software product:

i. Management moves two experienced testers to another project
ii. The system does not comply with security standards
iii. System response time exceeds user requirements
iv. Stakeholders have inaccurate expectations
v. Disabled people have problems when using the system

Which of them are project risks?

options.text: i, iv are project risks
iv, v are project risks
i, iii are project risks
ii, v are project risks

rationales.a: Correct. i (moving testers) and iv (inaccurate stakeholder expectations) are project risks. ii, iii, v are product risks.
rationales.b: Incorrect. v is a product risk (accessibility).
rationales.c: Incorrect. iii is a product risk (performance).
rationales.d: Incorrect. Both are product risks.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of how product risk analysis influences thoroughness and scope of testing?

options.text: The test manager monitors and reports the level of all known risks on a daily basis so the stakeholders can make an informed decision on the release date
One of the identified risks was "Lack of support of open-source databases", so the team decided to integrate the system with an open-source database
During the quantitative risk analysis, the team estimated the total level of all identified risks and reported it as the total residual risk before testing
Risk assessment revealed a very high level of performance risks, so it was decided to perform detailed performance efficiency testing early in the SDLC

rationales.a: Incorrect. This is risk monitoring, not analysis-driven test scoping.
rationales.b: Incorrect. This is an architectural decision, not testing.
rationales.c: Incorrect. This is quantitative analysis output, not influence on test scope.
rationales.d: Correct. This shows how risk analysis impacts the thoroughness of testing (i.e., the level of detail).

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following options are common metrics used for reporting on the quality level of the test object?

options.text: Number of defects found during system testing
Total effort on test design divided by the number of designed test cases
Number of executed test procedures
Number of defects found divided by the size of a work product
Time needed to repair a defect

rationales.a: Correct. The number of defects found is related to the test object quality.
rationales.b: Incorrect. This measures test efficiency, not quality.
rationales.c: Incorrect. The number of executed test procedures doesn't tell us about quality; test results might.
rationales.d: Correct. Defect density (defects per size of work product) is related to quality.
rationales.e: Incorrect. Time to repair is a process metric.

questions.stem: Which of the following pieces of information contained in a test progress report is the LEAST useful for business representatives?

options.text: Impediments to testing
Branch coverage achieved
Test progress
New risks within the test cycle

rationales.a: Incorrect. Impediments can be high-level and business-related — useful.
rationales.b: Correct. Branch testing is a technical metric used by developers and technical test analysts. Of no interest to business representatives.
rationales.c: Incorrect. Test progress is project-related, useful for business reps.
rationales.d: Incorrect. Risks impact product quality, useful for business reps.
